questions.question: What is the primary goal of synchronization in the critical section problem?
options: To prevent concurrent access to shared resources
questions.explanation: Synchronization in the critical section problem aims to prevent multiple processes from accessing and modifying shared resources simultaneously, thereby preventing data inconsistencies and system failures.

questions.question: Which type of synchronization technique is used to ensure that only one process can execute a critical section of code?
questions.explanation: A mutex (short for mutual exclusion) is a synchronization primitive that allows only one process to access a critical section of code at a time, preventing other processes from executing the same code simultaneously.

questions.question: What is a common consequence of not using synchronization in the critical section problem?
options: Increased CPU utilization and system throughput
questions.explanation: Not using synchronization in the critical section problem can lead to data loss and corruption, as multiple processes may access and modify shared resources simultaneously, resulting in inconsistent and unreliable data.

questions.question: Which algorithm is commonly used to resolve conflicts in the critical section problem?
questions.explanation: Dekker's Token Ring Protocol is a well-known algorithm that resolves conflicts in the critical section problem by distributing a token among processes, allowing only the process holding the token to access the critical section of code.

questions.question: What is the benefit of using synchronization in the critical section problem?
questions.explanation: Using synchronization in the critical section problem ensures that only one process can access and modify shared resources at a time, preventing data inconsistencies and ensuring system reliability, thereby improving system responsiveness.
